It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: Player at rows 36-38, cols 24-27. W bar on BOTTOM (row 39). Charcoal frames at rows 20-23 cols 28-31 and rows 24-27 cols 36-39 (both above and RIGHT). Need W bar to point RIGHT to shoot charcoal frames. Move UP to get closer to charcoal frame row range, then RIGHT to get blocked and switch W bar.
- Plan: First, inspect the board segmentation to understand all objects, their positions, colors, and relationships. Then probe with movement actions to understand the game mechanics.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 183 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 2.6x over the target.

sections.content: The player moved UP. Let me check the current state to see where the player is now and what the W bar position is. The animation_changed_bbox is [20, 5, 63, 27], which is a large region spanning rows 20-63 and cols 5-27. This suggests the player moved UP significantly.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g', 'G']:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"{n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check ascii around player and frames
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nRegion (rows 18-35, cols 22-42):")
for i in range(18, 35):
    print(f"{i:2d}: {ascii_lines[i][22:42]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player moved UP to rows 21-23, cols 24-27. The W bar is at row 20, cols 24-27 (ABOVE the player). So the W bar is now on the TOP side.
The charcoal frames are:
1. Top frame at rows 20-23, cols 28-31 (RIGHT of player)
2. Middle frame at rows 24-27, cols 36-39 (RIGHT and below player)
The W bar is on the TOP side (pointing UP). So the SPACE shot will hit frames above the player. But the charcoal frames are to the RIGHT of the player, not above.
To hit the charcoal frames, I need the W bar to point RIGHT. To switch the W bar to the RIGHT side, I need to move RIGHT and get blocked.
The player is at cols 24-27, and the top charcoal frame is at cols 28-31. So the top charcoal frame is RIGHT of the player. If I move RIGHT, I'll hit the top charcoal frame, which will switch the W bar to the RIGHT side.
Wait, but if I move RIGHT and hit the charcoal frame, the W bar will switch to the RIGHT side. Then I can shoot SPACE to convert the charcoal frame.
But actually, looking at the ascii, the top charcoal frame is at cols 28-31, which is RIGHT of the player (cols 24-27). So if I move RIGHT, I'll hit the charcoal frame.
Let me try moving RIGHT to get blocked by the charcoal frame, which should switch the W bar to the RIGHT side.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: Player at rows 21-23, cols 24-27. W bar on TOP (row 20). Charcoal frame at rows 20-23, cols 28-31 (RIGHT of player). Move RIGHT to get blocked by charcoal frame, which should switch W bar to RIGHT side. Then shoot SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move RIGHT to get blocked by charcoal frame
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
